narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 61.9% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admission decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.40 and LSAT score of 41, alongside your Year 1 and Year 3 class deciles of 2 and 1, your middle family income bracket, and your plan to attend full-time. Among students who passed the bar, the average undergraduate GPA is 3.24, and their Year 1 and Year 3 deciles average around 6 and 5.9, respectively, showing how your earlier academic rankings stand out as lower than typical for successful examinees. The three most influential factors in pushing your prediction toward failure were your Year 3 class decile, your LSAT score, and your Year 1 class decile. Most significantly, your low Year 3 decile strongly signaled a higher risk of bar failure, as it deviated notably from the stronger mid-to-upper deciles common among those who passed. In contrast, your above-average LSAT score of 41—higher than the 37.27 average for bar passers—worked in your favor, pulling the prediction toward success and nearly offsetting the top concern. Your Year 1 decile also contributed toward the failure risk, though with less force than Year 3, reinforcing patterns seen in students who struggled on the exam. Compared to typical admitted students, who often have higher deciles and balanced profiles, your combination tipped the scales despite strengths like your LSAT and full-time commitment, which aligns with 93% of bar passers. This isn't a judgment on your potential, but a data-driven caution based on historical outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it further or strengthening your academic deciles through additional coursework or a post-bac program, then reapply with an updated profile. You might also seek targeted bar prep resources early, as many students in similar positions have succeeded by addressing these key areas. With focused effort, you can shift these indicators and build a stronger case for admission next time.
